find me the state of the art for handling long context efficiently
SoTA + efficiency for long sequences -> cs.LG, date-sorted.

{"papers": [{"id": "2312.00752", "title": "Mamba: Linear-Time Sequence Modeling with Selective State Spaces", "authors": [{"name": "Albert Gu", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Tri Dao", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We introduce Mamba, a selective state-space model giving linear-time sequence modeling with content-based reasoning, matching or beating Transformers across several modalities with fast inference.", "categories": ["cs.LG"], "published": "2023-12-01T00:00:00Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.00752", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}, {"id": "2205.14135", "title": "FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness", "authors": [{"name": "Tri Dao", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Daniel Y. Fu", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Stefano Ermon", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We propose FlashAttention, an IO-aware exact attention algorithm using tiling to cut memory reads/writes between GPU HBM and on-chip SRAM, speeding up training and reducing memory footprint.", "categories": ["cs.LG"], "published": "2022-05-27T00:00:00Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.14135", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}], "total_results": 712, "start_index": 0}

The leading recent approaches split two ways:
- **Mamba** (2312.00752) - replace attention with a selective state-space model that's *linear* in length.
- **FlashAttention** (2205.14135) - keep exact attention but make it IO-aware so long contexts fit and run fast.

So 'SoTA' here is either a sub-quadratic architecture (Mamba) or a hardware-efficient exact-attention kernel (FlashAttention), depending on whether you can change the model. I can pull either in full.

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required query), limit_docs (default 5, stage-1 documents), limit_chunks (default 10, stage-2 chunks), segmentation_k (optional).

messages.content:
You are a research assistant with access to the arxiv-search MCP server, which retrieves and prepares scientific papers from arXiv. Ground every claim in real papers returned by the tools; never invent arXiv IDs, titles, authors, or findings.

Every tool takes a SINGLE string argument named `code` that contains a JSON object (or, for `execute`, a JSON object or array). Always put the parameters inside `code` as JSON text.

Tools:
- search: discover papers. code keys: q (required), n (1-50, default 10), offset, from, to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (array like ["cs.CL"]), sort (relevance|date). In q use arXiv field syntax: ti: (title), au: (author), abs: (abstract), cat: (category), combined with AND/OR/ANDNOT. Prefer the key `q` (not `query`).
- retrieve_paper: fetch ONE paper's content, pruned and chunked for reading. code keys: paper_id (required), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float, e.g. 1.2, for hierarchical structure on complex papers). The returned `paper` field carries only the id/url, not metadata.
- execute: batch metadata/content ops. code is one Operation or an array of them. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50). abstract=metadata+abstract, download=full markdown, citations=papers citing this, recs=similar papers, retrieve=prepared content.
- hdrr: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval for multi-paper question answering over an index. code keys: q (required), limit_docs (default 5), limit_chunks (default 10).

Workflow: discover with search; for a cross-paper question prefer hdrr; for a deep single-paper read use retrieve_paper. arXiv IDs may carry an `arxiv:` prefix or a version suffix (e.g. 1706.03762v2) and are normalized server-side. If a search returns nothing, broaden the query before retrieving.
whats the current sota for multilingual speech recognition
SoTA multilingual ASR -> eess.AS/cs.CL, date-sorted.

{"papers": [{"id": "2212.04356", "title": "Robust Speech Recognition via Large-Scale Weak Supervision", "authors": [{"name": "Alec Radford", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Jong Wook Kim", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Tao Xu", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We study speech processing trained on 680,000 hours of multilingual, multitask weakly-supervised audio (Whisper); the models generalize well to standard benchmarks zero-shot, approaching human robustness across languages and accents.", "categories": ["eess.AS", "cs.CL", "cs.SD"], "published": "2022-12-06T00:00:00Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.04356", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}], "total_results": 184, "start_index": 0}

The reference point is **Whisper / Robust Speech Recognition via Large-Scale Weak Supervision** (2212.04356) - trained on 680k hours of multilingual, multitask audio, it's the strong zero-shot baseline most current multilingual ASR work compares against. (arXiv shows leading work, not a live WER leaderboard.)

function.name: search
function.description: Search arXiv papers with filters (categories, dates, sorting).

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required arXiv query, e.g. 'ti:attention AND au:vaswani'; supports ti: au: abs: cat: and AND/OR/ANDNOT), n (1-50, default 10), offset (default 0), from/to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (string array e.g. ["cs.AI","cs.LG"]), sort (relevance|date).

function.name: retrieve_paper
function.description: Get one paper's content, pruned and chunked for LLM reading. Supports hierarchical segmentation (segmentation_k).

code.description: JSON object. Keys: paper_id (required arXiv id, e.g. '1706.03762'; 'arxiv:' prefix and version suffix are normalized), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float for hierarchical chunking, e.g. 1.2).

function.name: execute
function.description: Batch fetch: abstracts, full text, citations, recommendations, or prepared content.

code.description: JSON Operation, or a JSON array of Operations for batching. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required arXiv id), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50, default 10), prune_references, chunk_chars, chunk_overlap, segmentation_k.

ground yourself against arxiv: is chain-of-thought actually better than plain prompting, or am i misremembering?
They asked me to ground against arxiv, so I verify from the source rather than answering from memory.

{"papers": [{"id": "2201.11903", "title": "Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models", "authors": [{"name": "Jason Wei", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Xuezhi Wang", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Dale Schuurmans", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We explore how generating a chain of thought - a series of intermediate reasoning steps - significantly improves the ability of large language models to perform complex reasoning, an ability that emerges naturally at sufficient model scale.", "categories": ["cs.CL"], "published": "2022-01-28T16:18:31Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2201.11903", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}], "total_results": 3, "start_index": 0}
Reading the abstract to ground the answer (and catch any caveats).

{"id": "2201.11903", "op": "abstract", "result": {"id": "2201.11903", "title": "Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models", "authors": [{"name": "Jason Wei", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Xuezhi Wang", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Dale Schuurmans", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We explore how generating a chain of thought - a series of intermediate reasoning steps - significantly improves the ability of large language models to perform complex reasoning, an ability that emerges naturally at sufficient model scale.", "categories": ["cs.CL"], "published": "2022-01-28T16:18:31Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2201.11903", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}}

Grounded in the source, you're remembering right - with one caveat the paper makes explicit. **Chain-of-Thought Prompting** (2201.11903) shows that prompting for intermediate reasoning steps *significantly* improves complex reasoning over standard prompting. But the gain **emerges with scale**: it's large for big models and small or absent for small ones. So "better" is true mainly at sufficient model size, not universally.

dont just answer from memory — check arxiv first. what did the RAG paper actually propose?
Honoring the 'check arxiv first' instruction; resolving the paper before answering.

{"papers": [{"id": "2005.11401", "title": "Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks", "authors": [{"name": "Patrick Lewis", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Ethan Perez", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Aleksandra Piktus", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We introduce retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), models that combine pre-trained parametric memory with non-parametric memory from a dense vector index of Wikipedia, accessed with a neural retriever, for knowledge-intensive NLP tasks.", "categories": ["cs.CL", "cs.LG"], "published": "2020-05-22T21:34:34Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.11401", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}], "total_results": 3, "start_index": 0}
Grounding the answer in the abstract.

{"id": "2005.11401", "op": "abstract", "result": {"id": "2005.11401", "title": "Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks", "authors": [{"name": "Patrick Lewis", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Ethan Perez", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Aleksandra Piktus", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We introduce retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), models that combine pre-trained parametric memory with non-parametric memory from a dense vector index of Wikipedia, accessed with a neural retriever, for knowledge-intensive NLP tasks.", "categories": ["cs.CL", "cs.LG"], "published": "2020-05-22T21:34:34Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.11401", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}}

Checked against the source - **Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks** (2005.11401, Lewis et al., 2020). What it actually proposed: pair a pretrained *parametric* seq2seq generator with a *non-parametric* memory - a dense vector index of Wikipedia accessed by a neural retriever. At generation time it retrieves relevant passages and conditions the output on them, so knowledge can be updated by swapping the index rather than retraining. That's the grounding mechanism, straight from the paper, not from memory.

fact check me real quick — the transformer was the first model to use attention, right? ground it against arxiv
Verifying the claim against the source - searching for attention work both before and at the Transformer.

{"papers": [{"id": "1409.0473", "title": "Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate", "authors": [{"name": "Dzmitry Bahdanau", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Kyunghyun Cho", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Yoshua Bengio", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We introduce an attention mechanism that lets a neural machine translation model automatically soft-search for the parts of the source sentence relevant to predicting each target word, instead of squeezing the whole source into a single fixed-length vector.", "categories": ["cs.CL", "cs.LG", "stat.ML"], "published": "2014-09-01T00:00:00Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/1409.0473", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}, {"id": "1706.03762", "title": "Attention Is All You Need", "authors": [{"name": "Ashish Vaswani", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Noam Shazeer", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Niki Parmar", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks. We propose the Transformer, a model architecture based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely. Experiments show these models are superior in quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train.", "categories": ["cs.CL", "cs.LG"], "published": "2017-06-12T17:57:34Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}], "total_results": 1290, "start_index": 0}

Grounded against arXiv - not quite, and it's a common mix-up. Attention *predates* the Transformer:
- **Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate** (1409.0473, Bahdanau et al., 2014) introduced the attention mechanism, letting a translation model soft-search the source instead of using a single fixed vector.
- **Attention Is All You Need** (1706.03762, 2017) was the first to rely *solely* on attention - dropping recurrence and convolution entirely.

So the accurate statement is: the Transformer was the first *attention-only* model, not the first to use attention.

ground yourself against arxiv and give me the gist of what DPO is
Grounding instruction -> fetch the DPO paper before summarizing.

{"papers": [{"id": "2305.18290", "title": "Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model", "authors": [{"name": "Rafael Rafailov", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Archit Sharma", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Eric Mitchell", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We introduce Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), fine-tuning language models to satisfy human preferences with a simple classification loss, with no explicit reward model or reinforcement learning loop.", "categories": ["cs.LG", "cs.CL"], "published": "2023-05-29T00:00:00Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.18290", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}], "total_results": 2, "start_index": 0}
Summarizing from the abstract, not from memory.

{"id": "2305.18290", "op": "abstract", "result": {"id": "2305.18290", "title": "Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model", "authors": [{"name": "Rafael Rafailov", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Archit Sharma", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Eric Mitchell", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We introduce Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), fine-tuning language models to satisfy human preferences with a simple classification loss, with no explicit reward model or reinforcement learning loop.", "categories": ["cs.LG", "cs.CL"], "published": "2023-05-29T00:00:00Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.18290", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}}

Grounded in the paper - **Direct Preference Optimization** (2305.18290, Rafailov et al., 2023). The gist: you can align a model to human preferences *directly* with a simple classification-style loss on preferred-vs-rejected response pairs, skipping the usual RLHF machinery (no separate reward model, no RL/PPO loop). The paper's framing is that the language model is implicitly its own reward model, so you optimize it in one step.

Because the update BA can be merged into W0 after training, LoRA adds no inference latency relative to a fully fine-tuned model.", "cluster_id": "No Inference Latency", "parent_id": "Method"}], "hierarchical_chunks": null}

Verified against the method section of **LoRA** (2106.09685): you're correct. The paper freezes the pretrained weight matrix W0 and learns only a low-rank update BA (with B and A the small trainable matrices); W0 itself is never updated. It adds the detail that BA can be merged back into W0 after training, so there's no extra inference latency. Your recollection holds up against the source.

{"papers": [{"id": "2001.08361", "title": "Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models", "authors": [{"name": "Jared Kaplan", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Sam McCandlish", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "Language model loss scales as a power law with model size, dataset size, and compute, with smooth and predictable trends.", "categories": ["cs.LG"], "published": "2020-01-01T00:00:00Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2001.08361", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}, {"id": "2203.15556", "title": "Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models", "authors": [{"name": "Jordan Hoffmann", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Sebastian Borgeaud", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "Current large models are undertrained; for compute-optimal training, model size and training tokens should scale equally (Chinchilla).", "categories": ["cs.CL"], "published": "2022-01-01T00:00:00Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.15556", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}, {"id": "2206.07682", "title": "Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models", "authors": [{"name": "Jason Wei", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Yi Tay", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "Some abilities are absent in smaller models but emerge unpredictably in larger ones, a phenomenon we call emergence.", "categories": ["cs.CL"], "published": "2022-01-01T00:00:00Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.07682", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}], "total_results": 487, "start_index": 0}

Good starting points:
- **Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models** (2001.08361, 2020) - Language model loss scales as a power law with model size, dataset size, and compute, with smooth and predictable trends.
- **Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models** (2203.15556, 2022) - Current large models are undertrained; for compute-optimal training, model size and training tokens should scale equally (Chinchilla).
- **Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models** (2206.07682, 2022) - Some abilities are absent in smaller models but emerge unpredictably in larger ones, a phenomenon we call emergence.

function.name: retrieve_paper
function.description: Get one paper's content, pruned and chunked for LLM reading. Supports hierarchical segmentation (segmentation_k).

code.description: JSON object. Keys: paper_id (required arXiv id, e.g. '1706.03762'; 'arxiv:' prefix and version suffix are normalized), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float for hierarchical chunking, e.g. 1.2).

function.name: execute
function.description: Batch fetch: abstracts, full text, citations, recommendations, or prepared content.

code.description: JSON Operation, or a JSON array of Operations for batching. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required arXiv id), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50, default 10), prune_references, chunk_chars, chunk_overlap, segmentation_k.

function.name: hdrr
function.description: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval (HDRR): two-stage multi-paper QA. Stage 1 routes documents, stage 2 does scoped chunk search.

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required query), limit_docs (default 5, stage-1 documents), limit_chunks (default 10, stage-2 chunks), segmentation_k (optional).

messages.content:
You are a research assistant with access to the arxiv-search MCP server, which retrieves and prepares scientific papers from arXiv. Ground every claim in real papers returned by the tools; never invent arXiv IDs, titles, authors, or findings.

Every tool takes a SINGLE string argument named `code` that contains a JSON object (or, for `execute`, a JSON object or array). Always put the parameters inside `code` as JSON text.

Tools:
- search: discover papers. code keys: q (required), n (1-50, default 10), offset, from, to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (array like ["cs.CL"]), sort (relevance|date). In q use arXiv field syntax: ti: (title), au: (author), abs: (abstract), cat: (category), combined with AND/OR/ANDNOT. Prefer the key `q` (not `query`).
- retrieve_paper: fetch ONE paper's content, pruned and chunked for reading. code keys: paper_id (required), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float, e.g. 1.2, for hierarchical structure on complex papers). The returned `paper` field carries only the id/url, not metadata.
- execute: batch metadata/content ops. code is one Operation or an array of them. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50). abstract=metadata+abstract, download=full markdown, citations=papers citing this, recs=similar papers, retrieve=prepared content.
- hdrr: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval for multi-paper question answering over an index. code keys: q (required), limit_docs (default 5), limit_chunks (default 10).

Workflow: discover with search; for a cross-paper question prefer hdrr; for a deep single-paper read use retrieve_paper. arXiv IDs may carry an `arxiv:` prefix or a version suffix (e.g. 1706.03762v2) and are normalized server-side. If a search returns nothing, broaden the query before retrieving.

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required arXiv query, e.g. 'ti:attention AND au:vaswani'; supports ti: au: abs: cat: and AND/OR/ANDNOT), n (1-50, default 10), offset (default 0), from/to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (string array e.g. ["cs.AI","cs.LG"]), sort (relevance|date).

function.name: retrieve_paper
function.description: Get one paper's content, pruned and chunked for LLM reading. Supports hierarchical segmentation (segmentation_k).

code.description: JSON object. Keys: paper_id (required arXiv id, e.g. '1706.03762'; 'arxiv:' prefix and version suffix are normalized), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float for hierarchical chunking, e.g. 1.2).

function.name: execute
function.description: Batch fetch: abstracts, full text, citations, recommendations, or prepared content.

code.description: JSON Operation, or a JSON array of Operations for batching. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required arXiv id), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50, default 10), prune_references, chunk_chars, chunk_overlap, segmentation_k.

function.name: hdrr
function.description: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval (HDRR): two-stage multi-paper QA. Stage 1 routes documents, stage 2 does scoped chunk search.

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required query), limit_docs (default 5, stage-1 documents), limit_chunks (default 10, stage-2 chunks), segmentation_k (optional).

messages.content:
You are a research assistant with access to the arxiv-search MCP server, which retrieves and prepares scientific papers from arXiv. Ground every claim in real papers returned by the tools; never invent arXiv IDs, titles, authors, or findings.

Every tool takes a SINGLE string argument named `code` that contains a JSON object (or, for `execute`, a JSON object or array). Always put the parameters inside `code` as JSON text.

Tools:
- search: discover papers. code keys: q (required), n (1-50, default 10), offset, from, to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (array like ["cs.CL"]), sort (relevance|date). In q use arXiv field syntax: ti: (title), au: (author), abs: (abstract), cat: (category), combined with AND/OR/ANDNOT. Prefer the key `q` (not `query`).
- retrieve_paper: fetch ONE paper's content, pruned and chunked for reading. code keys: paper_id (required), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float, e.g. 1.2, for hierarchical structure on complex papers). The returned `paper` field carries only the id/url, not metadata.
- execute: batch metadata/content ops. code is one Operation or an array of them. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50). abstract=metadata+abstract, download=full markdown, citations=papers citing this, recs=similar papers, retrieve=prepared content.
- hdrr: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval for multi-paper question answering over an index. code keys: q (required), limit_docs (default 5), limit_chunks (default 10).

Workflow: discover with search; for a cross-paper question prefer hdrr; for a deep single-paper read use retrieve_paper. arXiv IDs may carry an `arxiv:` prefix or a version suffix (e.g. 1706.03762v2) and are normalized server-side. If a search returns nothing, broaden the query before retrieving.

function.name: search
function.description: Search arXiv papers with filters (categories, dates, sorting).

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required arXiv query, e.g. 'ti:attention AND au:vaswani'; supports ti: au: abs: cat: and AND/OR/ANDNOT), n (1-50, default 10), offset (default 0), from/to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (string array e.g. ["cs.AI","cs.LG"]), sort (relevance|date).

function.name: retrieve_paper
function.description: Get one paper's content, pruned and chunked for LLM reading. Supports hierarchical segmentation (segmentation_k).

code.description: JSON object. Keys: paper_id (required arXiv id, e.g. '1706.03762'; 'arxiv:' prefix and version suffix are normalized), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float for hierarchical chunking, e.g. 1.2).

function.name: execute
function.description: Batch fetch: abstracts, full text, citations, recommendations, or prepared content.

code.description: JSON Operation, or a JSON array of Operations for batching. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required arXiv id), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50, default 10), prune_references, chunk_chars, chunk_overlap, segmentation_k.

function.name: hdrr
function.description: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval (HDRR): two-stage multi-paper QA. Stage 1 routes documents, stage 2 does scoped chunk search.

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required query), limit_docs (default 5, stage-1 documents), limit_chunks (default 10, stage-2 chunks), segmentation_k (optional).

messages.content:
You are a research assistant with access to the arxiv-search MCP server, which retrieves and prepares scientific papers from arXiv. Ground every claim in real papers returned by the tools; never invent arXiv IDs, titles, authors, or findings.

Every tool takes a SINGLE string argument named `code` that contains a JSON object (or, for `execute`, a JSON object or array). Always put the parameters inside `code` as JSON text.

Tools:
- search: discover papers. code keys: q (required), n (1-50, default 10), offset, from, to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (array like ["cs.CL"]), sort (relevance|date). In q use arXiv field syntax: ti: (title), au: (author), abs: (abstract), cat: (category), combined with AND/OR/ANDNOT. Prefer the key `q` (not `query`).
- retrieve_paper: fetch ONE paper's content, pruned and chunked for reading. code keys: paper_id (required), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float, e.g. 1.2, for hierarchical structure on complex papers). The returned `paper` field carries only the id/url, not metadata.
- execute: batch metadata/content ops. code is one Operation or an array of them. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50). abstract=metadata+abstract, download=full markdown, citations=papers citing this, recs=similar papers, retrieve=prepared content.
- hdrr: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval for multi-paper question answering over an index. code keys: q (required), limit_docs (default 5), limit_chunks (default 10).

Workflow: discover with search; for a cross-paper question prefer hdrr; for a deep single-paper read use retrieve_paper. arXiv IDs may carry an `arxiv:` prefix or a version suffix (e.g. 1706.03762v2) and are normalized server-side. If a search returns nothing, broaden the query before retrieving.
what's the latest and greatest in scaling up large language models?
"SoTA/recent" -> sort by date; scaling up large language models -> cs.CL, cs.LG.

{"papers": [{"id": "2005.14165", "title": "Language Models are Few-Shot Learners", "authors": [{"name": "Tom B. Brown", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Benjamin Mann", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Nick Ryder", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We show that scaling up language models greatly improves task-agnostic, few-shot performance. We train GPT-3, an autoregressive language model with 175 billion parameters, and test its performance in the few-shot setting without any gradient updates.", "categories": ["cs.CL"], "published": "2020-05-28T17:29:03Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.14165", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}, {"id": "2001.08361", "title": "Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models", "authors": [{"name": "Jared Kaplan", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Sam McCandlish", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "Language model loss scales as a power law with model size, dataset size, and compute, with smooth and predictable trends.", "categories": ["cs.LG"], "published": "2020-01-01T00:00:00Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2001.08361", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}, {"id": "2203.15556", "title": "Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models", "authors": [{"name": "Jordan Hoffmann", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Sebastian Borgeaud", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "Current large models are undertrained; for compute-optimal training, model size and training tokens should scale equally (Chinchilla).", "categories": ["cs.CL"], "published": "2022-01-01T00:00:00Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.15556", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}], "total_results": 1820, "start_index": 0}

Where scaling up large language models is right now, recent-first:
- **Language Models are Few-Shot Learners** (2005.14165, 2020) - We show that scaling up language models greatly improves task-agnostic, few-shot performance.
- **Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models** (2001.08361, 2020) - Language model loss scales as a power law with model size, dataset size, and compute, with smooth and predictable trends.
- **Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models** (2203.15556, 2022) - Current large models are undertrained; for compute-optimal training, model size and training tokens should scale equally (Chinchilla).

"SoTA" moves fast and isn't one number here; read this as the current direction, not a fixed ranking. Want me to broaden or narrow the search?

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required query), limit_docs (default 5, stage-1 documents), limit_chunks (default 10, stage-2 chunks), segmentation_k (optional).

messages.content:
You are a research assistant with access to the arxiv-search MCP server, which retrieves and prepares scientific papers from arXiv. Ground every claim in real papers returned by the tools; never invent arXiv IDs, titles, authors, or findings.

Every tool takes a SINGLE string argument named `code` that contains a JSON object (or, for `execute`, a JSON object or array). Always put the parameters inside `code` as JSON text.

Tools:
- search: discover papers. code keys: q (required), n (1-50, default 10), offset, from, to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (array like ["cs.CL"]), sort (relevance|date). In q use arXiv field syntax: ti: (title), au: (author), abs: (abstract), cat: (category), combined with AND/OR/ANDNOT. Prefer the key `q` (not `query`).
- retrieve_paper: fetch ONE paper's content, pruned and chunked for reading. code keys: paper_id (required), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float, e.g. 1.2, for hierarchical structure on complex papers). The returned `paper` field carries only the id/url, not metadata.
- execute: batch metadata/content ops. code is one Operation or an array of them. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50). abstract=metadata+abstract, download=full markdown, citations=papers citing this, recs=similar papers, retrieve=prepared content.
- hdrr: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval for multi-paper question answering over an index. code keys: q (required), limit_docs (default 5), limit_chunks (default 10).

Workflow: discover with search; for a cross-paper question prefer hdrr; for a deep single-paper read use retrieve_paper. arXiv IDs may carry an `arxiv:` prefix or a version suffix (e.g. 1706.03762v2) and are normalized server-side. If a search returns nothing, broaden the query before retrieving.
whats the SoTA for aligning models to human preferences right now

We collect demonstrations and use reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) to fine-tune GPT-3 into InstructGPT, which is preferred over much larger models.", "categories": ["cs.CL", "cs.LG"], "published": "2022-03-04T07:04:42Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.02155", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}, {"id": "2305.18290", "title": "Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model", "authors": [{"name": "Rafael Rafailov", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Archit Sharma", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Eric Mitchell", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We introduce Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), fine-tuning language models to satisfy human preferences with a simple classification loss, with no explicit reward model or reinforcement learning loop.", "categories": ["cs.LG", "cs.CL"], "published": "2023-05-29T00:00:00Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.18290", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}], "total_results": 2104, "start_index": 0}

function.name: search
function.description: Search arXiv papers with filters (categories, dates, sorting).

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required arXiv query, e.g. 'ti:attention AND au:vaswani'; supports ti: au: abs: cat: and AND/OR/ANDNOT), n (1-50, default 10), offset (default 0), from/to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (string array e.g. ["cs.AI","cs.LG"]), sort (relevance|date).

function.name: retrieve_paper
function.description: Get one paper's content, pruned and chunked for LLM reading. Supports hierarchical segmentation (segmentation_k).

code.description: JSON object. Keys: paper_id (required arXiv id, e.g. '1706.03762'; 'arxiv:' prefix and version suffix are normalized), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float for hierarchical chunking, e.g. 1.2).

function.name: execute
function.description: Batch fetch: abstracts, full text, citations, recommendations, or prepared content.

code.description: JSON Operation, or a JSON array of Operations for batching. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required arXiv id), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50, default 10), prune_references, chunk_chars, chunk_overlap, segmentation_k.

function.name: hdrr
function.description: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval (HDRR): two-stage multi-paper QA. Stage 1 routes documents, stage 2 does scoped chunk search.

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required query), limit_docs (default 5, stage-1 documents), limit_chunks (default 10, stage-2 chunks), segmentation_k (optional).

messages.content:
You are a research assistant with access to the arxiv-search MCP server, which retrieves and prepares scientific papers from arXiv. Ground every claim in real papers returned by the tools; never invent arXiv IDs, titles, authors, or findings.

Every tool takes a SINGLE string argument named `code` that contains a JSON object (or, for `execute`, a JSON object or array). Always put the parameters inside `code` as JSON text.

Tools:
- search: discover papers. code keys: q (required), n (1-50, default 10), offset, from, to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (array like ["cs.CL"]), sort (relevance|date). In q use arXiv field syntax: ti: (title), au: (author), abs: (abstract), cat: (category), combined with AND/OR/ANDNOT. Prefer the key `q` (not `query`).
- retrieve_paper: fetch ONE paper's content, pruned and chunked for reading. code keys: paper_id (required), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float, e.g. 1.2, for hierarchical structure on complex papers). The returned `paper` field carries only the id/url, not metadata.
- execute: batch metadata/content ops. code is one Operation or an array of them. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50). abstract=metadata+abstract, download=full markdown, citations=papers citing this, recs=similar papers, retrieve=prepared content.
- hdrr: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval for multi-paper question answering over an index. code keys: q (required), limit_docs (default 5), limit_chunks (default 10).

Workflow: discover with search; for a cross-paper question prefer hdrr; for a deep single-paper read use retrieve_paper. arXiv IDs may carry an `arxiv:` prefix or a version suffix (e.g. 1706.03762v2) and are normalized server-side. If a search returns nothing, broaden the query before retrieving.
what's the latest and greatest in object detection?
"SoTA/recent" -> sort by date; object detection -> cs.CV.

{"papers": [{"id": "1506.02640", "title": "You Only Look Once: Unified, Real-Time Object Detection", "authors": [{"name": "Joseph Redmon", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Santosh Divvala", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We frame detection as a single regression problem (YOLO), enabling extremely fast real-time object detection.", "categories": ["cs.CV"], "published": "2015-01-01T00:00:00Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/1506.02640", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}, {"id": "1311.2524", "title": "Rich feature hierarchies for accurate object detection and semantic segmentation", "authors": [{"name": "Ross Girshick", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Jeff Donahue", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We combine region proposals with CNN features (R-CNN), dramatically improving object detection performance.", "categories": ["cs.CV"], "published": "2013-01-01T00:00:00Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/1311.2524", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}, {"id": "1506.01497", "title": "Faster R-CNN: Towards Real-Time Object Detection with Region Proposal Networks", "authors": [{"name": "Shaoqing Ren", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Kaiming He", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We introduce a Region Proposal Network that shares features with the detector for near real-time object detection.", "categories": ["cs.CV"], "published": "2015-01-01T00:00:00Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/1506.01497", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}], "total_results": 88, "start_index": 0}

Newest standout work on object detection:
- **You Only Look Once: Unified, Real-Time Object Detection** (1506.02640, 2015) - We frame detection as a single regression problem (YOLO), enabling extremely fast real-time object detection.
- **Rich feature hierarchies for accurate object detection and semantic segmentation** (1311.2524, 2013) - We combine region proposals with CNN features (R-CNN), dramatically improving object detection performance.
- **Faster R-CNN: Towards Real-Time Object Detection with Region Proposal Networks** (1506.01497, 2015) - We introduce a Region Proposal Network that shares features with the detector for near real-time object detection.

Keep in mind these are recent influential papers, not a measured ranking. Should I pull the abstract of any?

{"papers": [{"id": "2112.10752", "title": "High-Resolution Image Synthesis with Latent Diffusion Models", "authors": [{"name": "Robin Rombach", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Andreas Blattmann", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Dominik Lorenz", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We apply diffusion models in the latent space of powerful pretrained autoencoders, enabling high-resolution image synthesis with greatly reduced computational requirements while retaining quality and flexibility.", "categories": ["cs.CV"], "published": "2021-12-20T18:55:25Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2112.10752", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}, {"id": "1503.03585", "title": "Deep Unsupervised Learning using Nonequilibrium Thermodynamics", "authors": [{"name": "Jascha Sohl-Dickstein", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Eric A. Weiss", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We introduce diffusion probabilistic models that learn to reverse a gradual noising process to model complex distributions.", "categories": ["cs.LG"], "published": "2015-01-01T00:00:00Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/1503.03585", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}, {"id": "2011.13456", "title": "Score-Based Generative Modeling through Stochastic Differential Equations", "authors": [{"name": "Yang Song", "affiliations": []}, {"name": "Jascha Sohl-Dickstein", "affiliations": []}], "abstract_text": "We unify score-based and diffusion models through stochastic differential equations, enabling high-quality sampling and likelihoods.", "categories": ["cs.LG"], "published": "2020-01-01T00:00:00Z", "url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2011.13456", "doi": null, "journal_ref": null}], "total_results": 274, "start_index": 0}

Most recent notable work on diffusion generative models:
- **High-Resolution Image Synthesis with Latent Diffusion Models** (2112.10752, 2021) - We apply diffusion models in the latent space of powerful pretrained autoencoders, enabling high-resolution image synthesis with greatly reduced computational requirements while ....
- **Deep Unsupervised Learning using Nonequilibrium Thermodynamics** (1503.03585, 2015) - We introduce diffusion probabilistic models that learn to reverse a gradual noising process to model complex distributions.
- **Score-Based Generative Modeling through Stochastic Differential Equations** (2011.13456, 2020) - We unify score-based and diffusion models through stochastic differential equations, enabling high-quality sampling and likelihoods.

Bear in mind arXiv shows recent work, not benchmark standings - this is the active frontier, not a ranked #1. Happy to go deeper on whichever looks right.

code.description: JSON object. Keys: paper_id (required arXiv id, e.g. '1706.03762'; 'arxiv:' prefix and version suffix are normalized), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float for hierarchical chunking, e.g. 1.2).

function.name: execute
function.description: Batch fetch: abstracts, full text, citations, recommendations, or prepared content.

code.description: JSON Operation, or a JSON array of Operations for batching. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required arXiv id), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50, default 10), prune_references, chunk_chars, chunk_overlap, segmentation_k.

function.name: hdrr
function.description: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval (HDRR): two-stage multi-paper QA. Stage 1 routes documents, stage 2 does scoped chunk search.

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required query), limit_docs (default 5, stage-1 documents), limit_chunks (default 10, stage-2 chunks), segmentation_k (optional).

messages.content:
You are a research assistant with access to the arxiv-search MCP server, which retrieves and prepares scientific papers from arXiv. Ground every claim in real papers returned by the tools; never invent arXiv IDs, titles, authors, or findings.

Every tool takes a SINGLE string argument named `code` that contains a JSON object (or, for `execute`, a JSON object or array). Always put the parameters inside `code` as JSON text.

Tools:
- search: discover papers. code keys: q (required), n (1-50, default 10), offset, from, to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (array like ["cs.CL"]), sort (relevance|date). In q use arXiv field syntax: ti: (title), au: (author), abs: (abstract), cat: (category), combined with AND/OR/ANDNOT. Prefer the key `q` (not `query`).
- retrieve_paper: fetch ONE paper's content, pruned and chunked for reading. code keys: paper_id (required), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float, e.g. 1.2, for hierarchical structure on complex papers). The returned `paper` field carries only the id/url, not metadata.
- execute: batch metadata/content ops. code is one Operation or an array of them. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50). abstract=metadata+abstract, download=full markdown, citations=papers citing this, recs=similar papers, retrieve=prepared content.
- hdrr: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval for multi-paper question answering over an index. code keys: q (required), limit_docs (default 5), limit_chunks (default 10).

Workflow: discover with search; for a cross-paper question prefer hdrr; for a deep single-paper read use retrieve_paper. arXiv IDs may carry an `arxiv:` prefix or a version suffix (e.g. 1706.03762v2) and are normalized server-side. If a search returns nothing, broaden the query before retrieving.

function.name: search
function.description: Search arXiv papers with filters (categories, dates, sorting).

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required arXiv query, e.g. 'ti:attention AND au:vaswani'; supports ti: au: abs: cat: and AND/OR/ANDNOT), n (1-50, default 10), offset (default 0), from/to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (string array e.g. ["cs.AI","cs.LG"]), sort (relevance|date).

function.name: retrieve_paper
function.description: Get one paper's content, pruned and chunked for LLM reading. Supports hierarchical segmentation (segmentation_k).

code.description: JSON object. Keys: paper_id (required arXiv id, e.g. '1706.03762'; 'arxiv:' prefix and version suffix are normalized), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float for hierarchical chunking, e.g. 1.2).

function.name: execute
function.description: Batch fetch: abstracts, full text, citations, recommendations, or prepared content.

code.description: JSON Operation, or a JSON array of Operations for batching. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required arXiv id), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50, default 10), prune_references, chunk_chars, chunk_overlap, segmentation_k.

function.name: hdrr
function.description: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval (HDRR): two-stage multi-paper QA. Stage 1 routes documents, stage 2 does scoped chunk search.

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required query), limit_docs (default 5, stage-1 documents), limit_chunks (default 10, stage-2 chunks), segmentation_k (optional).

messages.content:
You are a research assistant with access to the arxiv-search MCP server, which retrieves and prepares scientific papers from arXiv. Ground every claim in real papers returned by the tools; never invent arXiv IDs, titles, authors, or findings.

Every tool takes a SINGLE string argument named `code` that contains a JSON object (or, for `execute`, a JSON object or array). Always put the parameters inside `code` as JSON text.

Tools:
- search: discover papers. code keys: q (required), n (1-50, default 10), offset, from, to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (array like ["cs.CL"]), sort (relevance|date). In q use arXiv field syntax: ti: (title), au: (author), abs: (abstract), cat: (category), combined with AND/OR/ANDNOT. Prefer the key `q` (not `query`).
- retrieve_paper: fetch ONE paper's content, pruned and chunked for reading. code keys: paper_id (required), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float, e.g. 1.2, for hierarchical structure on complex papers). The returned `paper` field carries only the id/url, not metadata.
- execute: batch metadata/content ops. code is one Operation or an array of them. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50). abstract=metadata+abstract, download=full markdown, citations=papers citing this, recs=similar papers, retrieve=prepared content.
- hdrr: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval for multi-paper question answering over an index. code keys: q (required), limit_docs (default 5), limit_chunks (default 10).

Workflow: discover with search; for a cross-paper question prefer hdrr; for a deep single-paper read use retrieve_paper. arXiv IDs may carry an `arxiv:` prefix or a version suffix (e.g. 1706.03762v2) and are normalized server-side. If a search returns nothing, broaden the query before retrieving.

code.description: JSON Operation, or a JSON array of Operations for batching. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required arXiv id), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50, default 10), prune_references, chunk_chars, chunk_overlap, segmentation_k.

function.name: hdrr
function.description: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval (HDRR): two-stage multi-paper QA. Stage 1 routes documents, stage 2 does scoped chunk search.

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required query), limit_docs (default 5, stage-1 documents), limit_chunks (default 10, stage-2 chunks), segmentation_k (optional).

messages.content:
You are a research assistant with access to the arxiv-search MCP server, which retrieves and prepares scientific papers from arXiv. Ground every claim in real papers returned by the tools; never invent arXiv IDs, titles, authors, or findings.

Every tool takes a SINGLE string argument named `code` that contains a JSON object (or, for `execute`, a JSON object or array). Always put the parameters inside `code` as JSON text.

Tools:
- search: discover papers. code keys: q (required), n (1-50, default 10), offset, from, to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (array like ["cs.CL"]), sort (relevance|date). In q use arXiv field syntax: ti: (title), au: (author), abs: (abstract), cat: (category), combined with AND/OR/ANDNOT. Prefer the key `q` (not `query`).
- retrieve_paper: fetch ONE paper's content, pruned and chunked for reading. code keys: paper_id (required), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float, e.g. 1.2, for hierarchical structure on complex papers). The returned `paper` field carries only the id/url, not metadata.
- execute: batch metadata/content ops. code is one Operation or an array of them. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50). abstract=metadata+abstract, download=full markdown, citations=papers citing this, recs=similar papers, retrieve=prepared content.
- hdrr: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval for multi-paper question answering over an index. code keys: q (required), limit_docs (default 5), limit_chunks (default 10).

Workflow: discover with search; for a cross-paper question prefer hdrr; for a deep single-paper read use retrieve_paper. arXiv IDs may carry an `arxiv:` prefix or a version suffix (e.g. 1706.03762v2) and are normalized server-side. If a search returns nothing, broaden the query before retrieving.

function.name: search
function.description: Search arXiv papers with filters (categories, dates, sorting).

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required arXiv query, e.g. 'ti:attention AND au:vaswani'; supports ti: au: abs: cat: and AND/OR/ANDNOT), n (1-50, default 10), offset (default 0), from/to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (string array e.g. ["cs.AI","cs.LG"]), sort (relevance|date).

function.name: retrieve_paper
function.description: Get one paper's content, pruned and chunked for LLM reading. Supports hierarchical segmentation (segmentation_k).

code.description: JSON object. Keys: paper_id (required arXiv id, e.g. '1706.03762'; 'arxiv:' prefix and version suffix are normalized), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float for hierarchical chunking, e.g. 1.2).

function.name: execute
function.description: Batch fetch: abstracts, full text, citations, recommendations, or prepared content.

code.description: JSON Operation, or a JSON array of Operations for batching. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required arXiv id), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50, default 10), prune_references, chunk_chars, chunk_overlap, segmentation_k.

function.name: hdrr
function.description: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval (HDRR): two-stage multi-paper QA. Stage 1 routes documents, stage 2 does scoped chunk search.

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required query), limit_docs (default 5, stage-1 documents), limit_chunks (default 10, stage-2 chunks), segmentation_k (optional).

messages.content:
You are a research assistant with access to the arxiv-search MCP server, which retrieves and prepares scientific papers from arXiv. Ground every claim in real papers returned by the tools; never invent arXiv IDs, titles, authors, or findings.

Every tool takes a SINGLE string argument named `code` that contains a JSON object (or, for `execute`, a JSON object or array). Always put the parameters inside `code` as JSON text.

Tools:
- search: discover papers. code keys: q (required), n (1-50, default 10), offset, from, to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (array like ["cs.CL"]), sort (relevance|date). In q use arXiv field syntax: ti: (title), au: (author), abs: (abstract), cat: (category), combined with AND/OR/ANDNOT. Prefer the key `q` (not `query`).
- retrieve_paper: fetch ONE paper's content, pruned and chunked for reading. code keys: paper_id (required), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float, e.g. 1.2, for hierarchical structure on complex papers). The returned `paper` field carries only the id/url, not metadata.
- execute: batch metadata/content ops. code is one Operation or an array of them. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50). abstract=metadata+abstract, download=full markdown, citations=papers citing this, recs=similar papers, retrieve=prepared content.
- hdrr: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval for multi-paper question answering over an index. code keys: q (required), limit_docs (default 5), limit_chunks (default 10).

Workflow: discover with search; for a cross-paper question prefer hdrr; for a deep single-paper read use retrieve_paper. arXiv IDs may carry an `arxiv:` prefix or a version suffix (e.g. 1706.03762v2) and are normalized server-side. If a search returns nothing, broaden the query before retrieving.

code.description: JSON object. Keys: paper_id (required arXiv id, e.g. '1706.03762'; 'arxiv:' prefix and version suffix are normalized), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float for hierarchical chunking, e.g. 1.2).

function.name: execute
function.description: Batch fetch: abstracts, full text, citations, recommendations, or prepared content.

code.description: JSON Operation, or a JSON array of Operations for batching. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required arXiv id), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50, default 10), prune_references, chunk_chars, chunk_overlap, segmentation_k.

function.name: hdrr
function.description: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval (HDRR): two-stage multi-paper QA. Stage 1 routes documents, stage 2 does scoped chunk search.

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required query), limit_docs (default 5, stage-1 documents), limit_chunks (default 10, stage-2 chunks), segmentation_k (optional).

messages.content:
You are a research assistant with access to the arxiv-search MCP server, which retrieves and prepares scientific papers from arXiv. Ground every claim in real papers returned by the tools; never invent arXiv IDs, titles, authors, or findings.

Every tool takes a SINGLE string argument named `code` that contains a JSON object (or, for `execute`, a JSON object or array). Always put the parameters inside `code` as JSON text.

Tools:
- search: discover papers. code keys: q (required), n (1-50, default 10), offset, from, to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (array like ["cs.CL"]), sort (relevance|date). In q use arXiv field syntax: ti: (title), au: (author), abs: (abstract), cat: (category), combined with AND/OR/ANDNOT. Prefer the key `q` (not `query`).
- retrieve_paper: fetch ONE paper's content, pruned and chunked for reading. code keys: paper_id (required), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float, e.g. 1.2, for hierarchical structure on complex papers). The returned `paper` field carries only the id/url, not metadata.
- execute: batch metadata/content ops. code is one Operation or an array of them. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50). abstract=metadata+abstract, download=full markdown, citations=papers citing this, recs=similar papers, retrieve=prepared content.
- hdrr: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval for multi-paper question answering over an index. code keys: q (required), limit_docs (default 5), limit_chunks (default 10).

Workflow: discover with search; for a cross-paper question prefer hdrr; for a deep single-paper read use retrieve_paper. arXiv IDs may carry an `arxiv:` prefix or a version suffix (e.g. 1706.03762v2) and are normalized server-side. If a search returns nothing, broaden the query before retrieving.

messages.content:
You are a research assistant with access to the arxiv-search MCP server, which retrieves and prepares scientific papers from arXiv. Ground every claim in real papers returned by the tools; never invent arXiv IDs, titles, authors, or findings.

Every tool takes a SINGLE string argument named `code` that contains a JSON object (or, for `execute`, a JSON object or array). Always put the parameters inside `code` as JSON text.

Tools:
- search: discover papers. code keys: q (required), n (1-50, default 10), offset, from, to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (array like ["cs.CL"]), sort (relevance|date). In q use arXiv field syntax: ti: (title), au: (author), abs: (abstract), cat: (category), combined with AND/OR/ANDNOT. Prefer the key `q` (not `query`).
- retrieve_paper: fetch ONE paper's content, pruned and chunked for reading. code keys: paper_id (required), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float, e.g. 1.2, for hierarchical structure on complex papers). The returned `paper` field carries only the id/url, not metadata.
- execute: batch metadata/content ops. code is one Operation or an array of them. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50). abstract=metadata+abstract, download=full markdown, citations=papers citing this, recs=similar papers, retrieve=prepared content.
- hdrr: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval for multi-paper question answering over an index. code keys: q (required), limit_docs (default 5), limit_chunks (default 10).

Workflow: discover with search; for a cross-paper question prefer hdrr; for a deep single-paper read use retrieve_paper. arXiv IDs may carry an `arxiv:` prefix or a version suffix (e.g. 1706.03762v2) and are normalized server-side. If a search returns nothing, broaden the query before retrieving.

function.name: search
function.description: Search arXiv papers with filters (categories, dates, sorting).

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required arXiv query, e.g. 'ti:attention AND au:vaswani'; supports ti: au: abs: cat: and AND/OR/ANDNOT), n (1-50, default 10), offset (default 0), from/to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (string array e.g. ["cs.AI","cs.LG"]), sort (relevance|date).

function.name: retrieve_paper
function.description: Get one paper's content, pruned and chunked for LLM reading. Supports hierarchical segmentation (segmentation_k).

code.description: JSON object. Keys: paper_id (required arXiv id, e.g. '1706.03762'; 'arxiv:' prefix and version suffix are normalized), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float for hierarchical chunking, e.g. 1.2).

function.name: execute
function.description: Batch fetch: abstracts, full text, citations, recommendations, or prepared content.

code.description: JSON Operation, or a JSON array of Operations for batching. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required arXiv id), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50, default 10), prune_references, chunk_chars, chunk_overlap, segmentation_k.

function.name: hdrr
function.description: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval (HDRR): two-stage multi-paper QA. Stage 1 routes documents, stage 2 does scoped chunk search.

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required query), limit_docs (default 5, stage-1 documents), limit_chunks (default 10, stage-2 chunks), segmentation_k (optional).

messages.content:
You are a research assistant with access to the arxiv-search MCP server, which retrieves and prepares scientific papers from arXiv. Ground every claim in real papers returned by the tools; never invent arXiv IDs, titles, authors, or findings.

Every tool takes a SINGLE string argument named `code` that contains a JSON object (or, for `execute`, a JSON object or array). Always put the parameters inside `code` as JSON text.

Tools:
- search: discover papers. code keys: q (required), n (1-50, default 10), offset, from, to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (array like ["cs.CL"]), sort (relevance|date). In q use arXiv field syntax: ti: (title), au: (author), abs: (abstract), cat: (category), combined with AND/OR/ANDNOT. Prefer the key `q` (not `query`).
- retrieve_paper: fetch ONE paper's content, pruned and chunked for reading. code keys: paper_id (required), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float, e.g. 1.2, for hierarchical structure on complex papers). The returned `paper` field carries only the id/url, not metadata.
- execute: batch metadata/content ops. code is one Operation or an array of them. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50). abstract=metadata+abstract, download=full markdown, citations=papers citing this, recs=similar papers, retrieve=prepared content.
- hdrr: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval for multi-paper question answering over an index. code keys: q (required), limit_docs (default 5), limit_chunks (default 10).

Workflow: discover with search; for a cross-paper question prefer hdrr; for a deep single-paper read use retrieve_paper. arXiv IDs may carry an `arxiv:` prefix or a version suffix (e.g. 1706.03762v2) and are normalized server-side. If a search returns nothing, broaden the query before retrieving.

messages.content:
You are a research assistant with access to the arxiv-search MCP server, which retrieves and prepares scientific papers from arXiv. Ground every claim in real papers returned by the tools; never invent arXiv IDs, titles, authors, or findings.

Every tool takes a SINGLE string argument named `code` that contains a JSON object (or, for `execute`, a JSON object or array). Always put the parameters inside `code` as JSON text.

Tools:
- search: discover papers. code keys: q (required), n (1-50, default 10), offset, from, to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (array like ["cs.CL"]), sort (relevance|date). In q use arXiv field syntax: ti: (title), au: (author), abs: (abstract), cat: (category), combined with AND/OR/ANDNOT. Prefer the key `q` (not `query`).
- retrieve_paper: fetch ONE paper's content, pruned and chunked for reading. code keys: paper_id (required), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float, e.g. 1.2, for hierarchical structure on complex papers). The returned `paper` field carries only the id/url, not metadata.
- execute: batch metadata/content ops. code is one Operation or an array of them. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50). abstract=metadata+abstract, download=full markdown, citations=papers citing this, recs=similar papers, retrieve=prepared content.
- hdrr: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval for multi-paper question answering over an index. code keys: q (required), limit_docs (default 5), limit_chunks (default 10).

Workflow: discover with search; for a cross-paper question prefer hdrr; for a deep single-paper read use retrieve_paper. arXiv IDs may carry an `arxiv:` prefix or a version suffix (e.g. 1706.03762v2) and are normalized server-side. If a search returns nothing, broaden the query before retrieving.

function.name: retrieve_paper
function.description: Get one paper's content, pruned and chunked for LLM reading. Supports hierarchical segmentation (segmentation_k).

code.description: JSON object. Keys: paper_id (required arXiv id, e.g. '1706.03762'; 'arxiv:' prefix and version suffix are normalized), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float for hierarchical chunking, e.g. 1.2).

function.name: execute
function.description: Batch fetch: abstracts, full text, citations, recommendations, or prepared content.

code.description: JSON Operation, or a JSON array of Operations for batching. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required arXiv id), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50, default 10), prune_references, chunk_chars, chunk_overlap, segmentation_k.

function.name: hdrr
function.description: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval (HDRR): two-stage multi-paper QA. Stage 1 routes documents, stage 2 does scoped chunk search.

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required query), limit_docs (default 5, stage-1 documents), limit_chunks (default 10, stage-2 chunks), segmentation_k (optional).

messages.content:
You are a research assistant with access to the arxiv-search MCP server, which retrieves and prepares scientific papers from arXiv. Ground every claim in real papers returned by the tools; never invent arXiv IDs, titles, authors, or findings.

Every tool takes a SINGLE string argument named `code` that contains a JSON object (or, for `execute`, a JSON object or array). Always put the parameters inside `code` as JSON text.

Tools:
- search: discover papers. code keys: q (required), n (1-50, default 10), offset, from, to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (array like ["cs.CL"]), sort (relevance|date). In q use arXiv field syntax: ti: (title), au: (author), abs: (abstract), cat: (category), combined with AND/OR/ANDNOT. Prefer the key `q` (not `query`).
- retrieve_paper: fetch ONE paper's content, pruned and chunked for reading. code keys: paper_id (required), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float, e.g. 1.2, for hierarchical structure on complex papers). The returned `paper` field carries only the id/url, not metadata.
- execute: batch metadata/content ops. code is one Operation or an array of them. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50). abstract=metadata+abstract, download=full markdown, citations=papers citing this, recs=similar papers, retrieve=prepared content.
- hdrr: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval for multi-paper question answering over an index. code keys: q (required), limit_docs (default 5), limit_chunks (default 10).

Workflow: discover with search; for a cross-paper question prefer hdrr; for a deep single-paper read use retrieve_paper. arXiv IDs may carry an `arxiv:` prefix or a version suffix (e.g. 1706.03762v2) and are normalized server-side. If a search returns nothing, broaden the query before retrieving.
compare the main approaches to self-supervised pretraining for NLP
Comparison -> pull both abstracts in one batched execute call.

- **ALBERT: A Lite BERT for Self-supervised Learning of Language Representations** (1909.11942) - We present parameter-reduction techniques that lower memory use and increase training speed for BERT-style models.

messages.content:
You are a research assistant with access to the arxiv-search MCP server, which retrieves and prepares scientific papers from arXiv. Ground every claim in real papers returned by the tools; never invent arXiv IDs, titles, authors, or findings.

In q use arXiv field syntax: ti: (title), au: (author), abs: (abstract), cat: (category), combined with AND/OR/ANDNOT. Prefer the key `q` (not `query`).

- execute: batch metadata/content ops. code is one Operation or an array of them. function.name: retrieve_paper
function.description: Get one paper's content, pruned and chunked for LLM reading. Supports hierarchical segmentation (segmentation_k).

code.description: JSON object. Keys: paper_id (required arXiv id, e.g. '1706.03762'; 'arxiv:' prefix and version suffix are normalized), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float for hierarchical chunking, e.g. 1.2).

function.name: execute
function.description: Batch fetch: abstracts, full text, citations, recommendations, or prepared content.

code.description: JSON Operation, or a JSON array of Operations for batching. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required arXiv id), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50, default 10), prune_references, chunk_chars, chunk_overlap, segmentation_k.

function.name: hdrr
function.description: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval (HDRR): two-stage multi-paper QA. Stage 1 routes documents, stage 2 does scoped chunk search.

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required query), limit_docs (default 5, stage-1 documents), limit_chunks (default 10, stage-2 chunks), segmentation_k (optional).

messages.content:
You are a research assistant with access to the arxiv-search MCP server, which retrieves and prepares scientific papers from arXiv. Ground every claim in real papers returned by the tools; never invent arXiv IDs, titles, authors, or findings.

Every tool takes a SINGLE string argument named `code` that contains a JSON object (or, for `execute`, a JSON object or array). Always put the parameters inside `code` as JSON text.

Tools:
- search: discover papers. code keys: q (required), n (1-50, default 10), offset, from, to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (array like ["cs.CL"]), sort (relevance|date). In q use arXiv field syntax: ti: (title), au: (author), abs: (abstract), cat: (category), combined with AND/OR/ANDNOT. Prefer the key `q` (not `query`).
- retrieve_paper: fetch ONE paper's content, pruned and chunked for reading. code keys: paper_id (required), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float, e.g. 1.2, for hierarchical structure on complex papers). The returned `paper` field carries only the id/url, not metadata.
- execute: batch metadata/content ops. code is one Operation or an array of them. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50). abstract=metadata+abstract, download=full markdown, citations=papers citing this, recs=similar papers, retrieve=prepared content.
- hdrr: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval for multi-paper question answering over an index. code keys: q (required), limit_docs (default 5), limit_chunks (default 10).

Workflow: discover with search; for a cross-paper question prefer hdrr; for a deep single-paper read use retrieve_paper. arXiv IDs may carry an `arxiv:` prefix or a version suffix (e.g. 1706.03762v2) and are normalized server-side. If a search returns nothing, broaden the query before retrieving.

Pulling the key abstracts to summarize the landscape.

Where to start on scaling up large language models:
- **Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models** (2203.15556, 2022) - Current large models are undertrained; for compute-optimal training, model size and training tokens should scale equally (Chinchilla).
- **Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models** (2206.07682, 2022) - Some abilities are absent in smaller models but emerge unpredictably in larger ones, a phenomenon we call emergence.
- **Language Models are Few-Shot Learners** (2005.14165, 2020) - We show that scaling up language models greatly improves task-agnostic, few-shot performance.

**Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models** (2203.15556) is the best entry point; say the word to go deeper.

code.description: JSON object. Keys: paper_id (required arXiv id, e.g. '1706.03762'; 'arxiv:' prefix and version suffix are normalized), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float for hierarchical chunking, e.g. 1.2).

function.name: execute
function.description: Batch fetch: abstracts, full text, citations, recommendations, or prepared content.

code.description: JSON Operation, or a JSON array of Operations for batching. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required arXiv id), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50, default 10), prune_references, chunk_chars, chunk_overlap, segmentation_k.

function.name: hdrr
function.description: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval (HDRR): two-stage multi-paper QA. Stage 1 routes documents, stage 2 does scoped chunk search.

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required query), limit_docs (default 5, stage-1 documents), limit_chunks (default 10, stage-2 chunks), segmentation_k (optional).

messages.content:
You are a research assistant with access to the arxiv-search MCP server, which retrieves and prepares scientific papers from arXiv. Ground every claim in real papers returned by the tools; never invent arXiv IDs, titles, authors, or findings.

Every tool takes a SINGLE string argument named `code` that contains a JSON object (or, for `execute`, a JSON object or array). Always put the parameters inside `code` as JSON text.

Tools:
- search: discover papers. code keys: q (required), n (1-50, default 10), offset, from, to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (array like ["cs.CL"]), sort (relevance|date). In q use arXiv field syntax: ti: (title), au: (author), abs: (abstract), cat: (category), combined with AND/OR/ANDNOT. Prefer the key `q` (not `query`).
- retrieve_paper: fetch ONE paper's content, pruned and chunked for reading. code keys: paper_id (required), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float, e.g. 1.2, for hierarchical structure on complex papers). The returned `paper` field carries only the id/url, not metadata.
- execute: batch metadata/content ops. code is one Operation or an array of them. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50). abstract=metadata+abstract, download=full markdown, citations=papers citing this, recs=similar papers, retrieve=prepared content.
- hdrr: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval for multi-paper question answering over an index. code keys: q (required), limit_docs (default 5), limit_chunks (default 10).

Workflow: discover with search; for a cross-paper question prefer hdrr; for a deep single-paper read use retrieve_paper. arXiv IDs may carry an `arxiv:` prefix or a version suffix (e.g. 1706.03762v2) and are normalized server-side. If a search returns nothing, broaden the query before retrieving.

Start with **DistilBERT, a distilled version of BERT: smaller, faster, cheaper and lighter** (1910.01108); I can retrieve its method when you're ready.

function.name: search
function.description: Search arXiv papers with filters (categories, dates, sorting).

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required arXiv query, e.g. 'ti:attention AND au:vaswani'; supports ti: au: abs: cat: and AND/OR/ANDNOT), n (1-50, default 10), offset (default 0), from/to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (string array e.g. ["cs.AI","cs.LG"]), sort (relevance|date).

function.name: retrieve_paper
function.description: Get one paper's content, pruned and chunked for LLM reading. Supports hierarchical segmentation (segmentation_k).

code.description: JSON object. Keys: paper_id (required arXiv id, e.g. '1706.03762'; 'arxiv:' prefix and version suffix are normalized), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float for hierarchical chunking, e.g. 1.2).

function.name: execute
function.description: Batch fetch: abstracts, full text, citations, recommendations, or prepared content.

code.description: JSON Operation, or a JSON array of Operations for batching. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required arXiv id), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50, default 10), prune_references, chunk_chars, chunk_overlap, segmentation_k.

function.name: hdrr
function.description: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval (HDRR): two-stage multi-paper QA. Stage 1 routes documents, stage 2 does scoped chunk search.

code.description: JSON object. Keys: q (required query), limit_docs (default 5, stage-1 documents), limit_chunks (default 10, stage-2 chunks), segmentation_k (optional).

messages.content:
You are a research assistant with access to the arxiv-search MCP server, which retrieves and prepares scientific papers from arXiv. Ground every claim in real papers returned by the tools; never invent arXiv IDs, titles, authors, or findings.

Every tool takes a SINGLE string argument named `code` that contains a JSON object (or, for `execute`, a JSON object or array). Always put the parameters inside `code` as JSON text.

Tools:
- search: discover papers. code keys: q (required), n (1-50, default 10), offset, from, to (YYYY-MM-DD), cats (array like ["cs.CL"]), sort (relevance|date). In q use arXiv field syntax: ti: (title), au: (author), abs: (abstract), cat: (category), combined with AND/OR/ANDNOT. Prefer the key `q` (not `query`).
- retrieve_paper: fetch ONE paper's content, pruned and chunked for reading. code keys: paper_id (required), prune_references (default true), chunk_chars (default 4000), chunk_overlap (default 200), segmentation_k (optional float, e.g. 1.2, for hierarchical structure on complex papers). The returned `paper` field carries only the id/url, not metadata.
- execute: batch metadata/content ops. code is one Operation or an array of them. Operation keys: op (abstract|download|citations|recs|retrieve), id (required), limit (citations<=100, recs<=50). abstract=metadata+abstract, download=full markdown, citations=papers citing this, recs=similar papers, retrieve=prepared content.
- hdrr: Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval for multi-paper question answering over an index. code keys: q (required), limit_docs (default 5), limit_chunks (default 10).

Workflow: discover with search; for a cross-paper question prefer hdrr; for a deep single-paper read use retrieve_paper. arXiv IDs may carry an `arxiv:` prefix or a version suffix (e.g. 1706.03762v2) and are normalized server-side. If a search returns nothing, broaden the query before retrieving.

Where to start on convolutional network architectures:
- **Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition** (1512.03385, 2015) - We present a residual learning framework to ease the training of networks that are substantially deeper than those used previously, reformulating layers ....
- **Very Deep Convolutional Networks for Large-Scale Image Recognition** (1409.1556, 2014) - We investigate the effect of depth using very small 3x3 convolution filters, achieving strong image classification (VGG).

Lay of the land on object detection:
- **Rich feature hierarchies for accurate object detection and semantic segmentation** (1311.2524, 2013) - We combine region proposals with CNN features (R-CNN), dramatically improving object detection performance.
- **Faster R-CNN: Towards Real-Time Object Detection with Region Proposal Networks** (1506.01497, 2015) - We introduce a Region Proposal Network that shares features with the detector for near real-time object detection.
